name: Safe Room
description: A protected location where monsters, decay pressure, or pursuit rules are reduced so players can save, recover, manage inventory, or plan.
design_notes: Safe rooms are tension punctuation, not only utility rooms. They need a clear boundary, consistent monster exclusion, save or checkpoint rules, resource access, and abuse prevention. The room's audio, lighting, and UI language should tell players they are safe without breaking the fiction.
design_purpose: Give players relief and planning space after high-pressure horror sequences.
Provide a readable boundary for saving, checkpointing, inventory management, or recovery.
Let level designers control pacing between danger, respite, and renewed exposure.
player_fantasy: The player feels a rare moment of safety before stepping back into danger.

parameters.name: safe_room_id
parameters.description: Stable zone id used for save, AI, and pacing rules.

parameters.name: safe_room_boundary
parameters.description: Boundary definition that enters and exits sanctuary state.

parameters.name: monster_exclusion_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule controlling how threats react to the safe boundary.

parameters.name: checkpoint_restore_state
parameters.description: State restored when the player reloads from the room.

parameters.name: recovery_source_id
parameters.description: Optional room service that restores or manages resources.

parameters.name: abuse_prevention_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule preventing repeated boundary crossing from erasing danger.

edge_cases: The monster reaches the doorway exactly as the player crosses the safe_room_boundary.
A scripted event temporarily disables safety and must communicate the exception clearly.
The player saves after spending resources but before storage or recovery state commits.
A safe room borders a chase arena and allows repeated boundary abuse.
A checkpoint restore places the player in the room while monsters still remember last_known_player_position.
common_bugs: AI pathing targets a player inside the safe room and gets stuck at the boundary.
Safe room music starts but monster exclusion does not, creating contradictory cues.
Storage inventory commits independently from checkpoint data and duplicates or loses items.
Threat state resets every time the player touches the boundary.
Recovery sources restore on every reload instead of using persisted room service state.
balancing_notes: Safe rooms should be close enough to reduce frustration but spaced to preserve route planning.
Unlimited recovery lowers fear; limited recovery or storage tradeoffs keep resource decisions meaningful.
Breaking safe-room rules can be powerful once, but repeated exceptions teach players not to trust the system.
accessibility_notes: Use clear audio, lighting, map labels, and text cues to identify safe-room state.
Make save and storage interactions confirmable and readable under stress.
Avoid using safe-room music stingers or flicker effects that conflict with reduced-intensity settings.

implementation_notes.unity: Define safe_room_id zones with trigger volumes and an explicit SafeRoomState component. On entry, update AI target rules, audio snapshots, checkpoint eligibility, and resource station availability from the committed boundary state.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Area nodes for safe_room_boundary and an autoload zone manager for current sanctuary state. Store checkpoint_restore_state and recovery source usage in save data so reloads do not refresh one-use services.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use volumes or level streaming zones with Gameplay Tags for safe-room state. AI controllers should query monster_exclusion_rule_id before path requests, and save systems should commit storage, player state, and room service state atomically.
implementation_notes.web: For Phaser or custom engines, mark safe rooms in map data and process entry/exit through a zone reducer. Keep monster exclusion, checkpoint state, and recovery usage in gameplay state rather than only in scene scripts.

relationships.reason: Safe rooms can pause or recover sanity to create pressure relief between horror sequences.
Safe rooms often pair with save or checkpoint feedback so players understand recovery state.
Safe rooms usually exclude active monster patrol entry by rule, boundary, or director state.

example_games.title: Resident Evil 2
example_games.note: Uses safe rooms for saving, storage, and relief from pursuit.

example_games.title: The Evil Within
example_games.note: Uses sanctuary-like spaces as pacing breaks and upgrade locations.
